summary:
The Plano cultures were a group of related Late Paleo-Indian traditions of the North American Great Plains and adjacent Rocky Mountain foothills, dated approximately 10,000–6,000 BCE, that emerged following the decline of the [[event:1283|Clovis culture]] and the extinction of Pleistocene megafauna. The Plano complex includes several named traditions — Agate Basin, Hell Gap, Cody, Frederick, and others — each defined primarily by distinctive styles of finely flaked lanceolate projectile points.

Plano hunters specialized in bison hunting on the now-extinct giant Bison antiquus, developing sophisticated communal hunting strategies including game drives, jumps (driving animals over cliffs), and surrounds. Their lanceolate projectile points — unfluted, often with carefully thinned bases for hafting — represent some of the most skillfully made stone tools in North American prehistory. Plano sites include large kill sites where dozens or hundreds of bison were slaughtered in single hunting episodes, as well as smaller camps and workshop sites where tools were manufactured and maintained.

The Plano cultures document the successful adaptation of early Americans to a post-megafauna Great Plains dominated by bison, developing hunting strategies and social cooperation that would continue to characterize Plains cultures up to the historical period. The [[event:1284|Folsom tradition]] was an immediate predecessor closely related to the Plano complex, and the two traditions together represent the classic Paleo-Indian bison-hunting culture of the Great Plains.

images.caption: A map of the Great Plains, shaded in transparent red.
